appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Echocardiogram (CPT 93306), Member with Stable Angina (ICD-10 I25.10). On behalf of our 67-year-old female member, we respectfully request reconsideration of the denial issued for the transthoracic echocardiogram dated this admission. The denial cites Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin 0119 §III.A on the basis that submitted records reflect stable symptoms over a 12-month interval. We disagree. Per Aetna CPB 0119 §III.B.2, repeat transthoracic echocardiography is medically necessary when there is documented change in clinical status, including new-onset dyspnea on exertion or a measurable decline in functional capacity. Office notes dated within the prior 30 days document a decline from NYHA Class I to Class II with new exertional dyspnea after one block of ambulation, plus an unexplained 4 lb weight gain. These findings constitute new symptoms under §III.B.2 and warrant reassessment of left ventricular function and valvular status. The ACC/AHA 2021 Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain §4.2 explicitly recommends repeat TTE (Class IIa, LOE B-NR) for patients with stable ischemic heart disease who develop new or worsening symptoms before escalating to advanced imaging or revascularization. Performing TTE first is the lower-cost, guideline-directed pathway and avoids unnecessary stress testing or catheterization. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we request reconsideration within the 30-day window from the date of the adverse determination. We have enclosed the complete office note, NYHA functional assessment, and prior echocardiogram report for comparison. We respectfully ask that the denial be overturned and coverage approved.
citations: ACC/AHA 2021 Chest Pain Guideline §4.2 Class IIa

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — CABG (CPT 33533), Member with Unstable Angina (ICD-10 I25.110). We request reconsideration of the denial issued for coronary artery bypass grafting for our 64-year-old male member admitted with crescendo rest angina. The denial under Aetna CPB 0286 cites absence of stress imaging within the prior 90 days as the basis for step-therapy non-compliance. We respectfully submit that the step-therapy criterion was satisfied through an alternative pathway expressly permitted in Aetna CPB 0286 §V.C, which states that diagnostic coronary angiography demonstrating left main disease ≥50% stenosis or three-vessel disease with proximal LAD involvement constitutes sufficient anatomic high-risk stratification in lieu of repeat non-invasive testing. The catheterization report dated this hospitalization documents 70% distal left main stenosis, 80% proximal LAD stenosis, 75% mid-circumflex stenosis, and 65% mid-RCA stenosis — anatomy that plainly meets the §V.C threshold for triple-vessel disease with left-main and proximal LAD involvement. The interventional cardiologist's note further documents a SYNTAX II score of 34, placing the lesion complexity in the high-risk range that favors surgical over percutaneous revascularization. The patient additionally presented with rest angina refractory to maximally tolerated medical therapy including aspirin 81 mg daily, high-intensity atorvastatin 80 mg, metoprolol succinate 100 mg, isosorbide mononitrate 60 mg, and ranolazine 1000 mg twice daily — satisfying the medical-therapy-failure prong of CPB 0286 §V.A. Documented adherence (pharmacy refill records enclosed) refutes any non-compliance argument. The ACC/AHA/SCAI 2021 Coronary Artery Revascularization Guideline §7.1.1 assigns Class I, LOE B-R recommendation for CABG in patients with significant left main disease to improve survival, and §7.1.3 supports CABG over PCI in multivessel disease with proximal LAD involvement, particularly when SYNTAX score >32. The 2021 guideline §3.4 also notes that for hemodynamically stable patients with high-risk anatomy on diagnostic catheterization, repeat non-invasive stress imaging is neither necessary nor clinically appropriate, as it merely delays definitive revascularization. Surgical revascularization is therefore the guideline-directed therapy. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file this appeal within the 30-day reconsideration window. Enclosed are the catheterization report with SYNTAX II calculation, the heart team consensus note dated the day of admission, the medication adherence summary from the pharmacy, and the ACC/AHA appropriateness criteria worksheet. We respectfully request the denial be overturned and coverage approved.

Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Diagnostic Cardiac Catheterization (CPT 93458), Member with Angina, Unspecified (ICD-10 I20.9). We request reconsideration of the denial which cited Aetna CPB 0353 §IV for absent pretest probability calculation. The submitted record in fact satisfies the documentation requirement under CPB 0353 §IV.B, which permits coverage when the pretest probability of obstructive CAD is intermediate-to-high per the ACC/AHA 2021 Chest Pain Guideline. The member is a 62-year-old male with typical anginal chest pain (substernal, exertional, relieved by rest), hypertension, hyperlipidemia (LDL 154), active tobacco use (40 pack-years), and a first-degree relative with MI at age 54. Applying the PROMISE/CONFIRM pretest probability model referenced in ACC/AHA 2021 Chest Pain Guideline §5.2 Table 5, this patient's pretest probability is 70% — well above the 15% intermediate threshold. Per CPB 0353 §IV.B, invasive coronary angiography is appropriate when pretest probability exceeds 50% with typical symptoms, bypassing the need for prior non-invasive imaging. Additionally, the ACC/AHA 2021 Guideline §6.4.2 assigns Class IIa, LOE B-NR recommendation for direct invasive angiography in patients with high pretest probability and ongoing symptoms despite anti-anginal therapy. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) this appeal is filed within the 30-day deadline. We have enclosed the H&P documenting symptom character, a calculated PROMISE score, and the medication list demonstrating failure of optimal medical therapy. We respectfully request the denial be overturned and coverage approved for the planned diagnostic catheterization.
citations: ACC/AHA 2021 Chest Pain Guideline §6.4.2 Class IIa

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — PCI with Stent (CPT 92928), Member with Unstable Angina (ICD-10 I25.110). We request reconsideration of the denial issued under Aetna CPB 0535, which cited 60% angiographic stenosis without functional assessment for our 59-year-old male member. We respectfully submit that the case meets the alternative coverage criterion expressly defined in CPB 0535 §IV.A.2, which provides for coverage when fractional flow reserve (FFR) is ≤0.80 or instantaneous wave-free ratio (iFR) is ≤0.89, even at intermediate angiographic severity. The catheterization report dated this admission documents an FFR value of 0.74 across the mid-LAD lesion, well below the 0.80 ischemic threshold. The functional assessment was performed at the time of diagnostic angiography using an FDA-approved pressure wire and is documented on page 3 of the cath report; it appears this page was inadvertently omitted from the initial claim submission and we have enclosed the complete report herewith, including the pressure-wire pullback tracing demonstrating a clear pressure drop across the lesion. ACC/AHA/SCAI 2021 Coronary Artery Revascularization Guideline §5.2.1 assigns Class I, LOE A recommendation for PCI of intermediate (40–70%) stenoses with FFR ≤0.80 in patients with stable ischemic heart disease, and the same recommendation extends to unstable presentations per §5.3. The 2018 FFR/iFR consensus document referenced in the guideline establishes 0.80 as the validated ischemic threshold based on the FAME, FAME-2, and DEFINE-FLAIR trials. The patient additionally presented with rest angina lasting 25 minutes, dynamic ST-segment depression of 1.5 mm in the lateral leads, and a positive troponin trend (peak high-sensitivity troponin 0.18 ng/mL with a delta of 0.14 ng/mL on serial draws), satisfying the NSTEMI criterion of CPB 0535 §IV.B and the Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction. ACC/AHA 2014 NSTE-ACS Guideline §4.4 (Class I, LOE A) recommends an early invasive strategy with revascularization for NSTEMI patients at intermediate or higher risk (GRACE score ≥140; this member's GRACE score is 152). The combination of NSTEMI presentation, GRACE-stratified high risk, and physiologically confirmed ischemia by FFR establishes clear medical necessity. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file this appeal within the 30-day reconsideration window. Enclosed: complete cath report with pressure-wire tracing, serial troponins, ECG strips, and GRACE risk calculation. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.

Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Stress Echocardiogram (CPT 93350), Member with Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure (ICD-10 I50.32). We request reconsideration of the denial citing absent prior authorization under Aetna CPB 0119. We respectfully invoke the urgent-care exception explicitly provided in Aetna CPB 0119 §VII.B, which waives the prior-authorization requirement when the ordering clinician documents that delay in imaging would jeopardize the member's life, health, or ability to regain maximum function — consistent with the urgent-care definition at 29 CFR 2560.503-1(m)(1). The 74-year-old female member presented to the ED with acute decompensated heart failure (BNP 1,840 pg/mL, baseline 290), new pulmonary edema on chest X-ray, and oxygen saturation of 88% on room air. The ordering cardiologist's note dated the day of service documents: 'Stress echo required emergently to evaluate for ischemic etiology before diuresis stabilization; delay would risk recurrent decompensation.' This satisfies the urgent-care standard. ACC/AHA 2022 Heart Failure Guideline §7.1.3 recommends (Class I, LOE B-NR) prompt assessment for ischemic etiology in patients with new or worsening HFpEF/HFmrEF presentation, and the 2022 guideline §6.2 notes that stress echocardiography is the preferred non-invasive modality in patients with adequate echocardiographic windows. We have enclosed the ED triage note, the cardiologist's urgency attestation, the BNP trend, and the imaging report. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) and (m)(1), we request reconsideration within the 30-day deadline and confirm that retrospective authorization is appropriate per Aetna CPB 0119 §VII.B. We respectfully ask that the denial be overturned.
citations: ACC/AHA 2022 Heart Failure Guideline §7.1.3 Class I

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (CPT 33361), Member with Nonrheumatic Aortic Valve Stenosis (ICD-10 I35.0). We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0286 citing absent multidisciplinary heart team consultation. The required heart team evaluation was in fact completed on the date 14 days prior to the planned procedure and documented in a formal multidisciplinary case conference note co-signed by interventional cardiology (Dr. A, intra-cardiac structural lead), cardiothoracic surgery (Dr. B, board-certified in adult cardiac surgery), cardiac anesthesiology (Dr. C), a heart-valve clinic coordinator, and a clinical pharmacist. This note appears to have been omitted from the initial claim submission and is enclosed in full herewith, including the dated and signed attestation page that confirms in-person attendance by all listed clinicians. The conference assessment assigned our 82-year-old male member an STS-PROM (Predicted Risk of Mortality) score of 9.4%, which corresponds to high surgical risk per CPB 0286 §III.A threshold of ≥8%. Adjunctive frailty assessment yielded an Essential Frailty Toolset (EFT) score of 6 (indicating significant frailty), a 5-meter gait speed of 7.2 seconds, and a Katz ADL score of 4/6 — collectively consistent with elevated surgical morbidity beyond what STS-PROM alone captures. The patient has severe symptomatic aortic stenosis with the following echocardiographic parameters: AVA 0.7 cm² by continuity equation, mean transvalvular gradient 48 mmHg, peak velocity 4.6 m/s, dimensionless index 0.19, and preserved LVEF of 56%. He has NYHA Class III dyspnea limiting him to less than one block of ambulation, two episodes of exertional presyncope in the prior month, and worsening angina with minimal exertion. These findings satisfy every prong of CPB 0286 §III.B severity criteria and §III.A symptom criteria. Under Aetna CPB 0286 §III.C, TAVR is covered for high-surgical-risk patients (STS-PROM ≥8%) with severe symptomatic AS following documented heart team review. ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §3.2.4 assigns Class I, LOE A recommendation for TAVR in symptomatic severe AS patients aged ≥80 years or with life expectancy <10 years (this member's age and frailty profile place him squarely in this cohort), and §3.2.5 supports transfemoral access (planned approach here) as the preferred TAVR delivery route. The PARTNER 2A and PARTNER 3 trials cited in the 2020 guideline establish non-inferiority and superiority of TAVR versus SAVR across the surgical-risk spectrum. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within the 30-day reconsideration window. Enclosed: the complete heart team conference note with attestation page, STS-PROM calculation worksheet, EFT frailty assessment, full echocardiogram report, gated CT for TAVR sizing, and the cardiology consult note. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.
citations: ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §3.2.4 Class I

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — AFib Catheter Ablation (CPT 93656), Member with Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation (ICD-10 I48.0). We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0353 citing no prior antiarrhythmic therapy. We respectfully invoke the first-line ablation criterion expressly recognized in the 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Atrial Fibrillation Guideline §6.3.2, which assigns Class I, LOE A recommendation for catheter ablation as initial rhythm-control strategy in selected patients with symptomatic paroxysmal AF, particularly those who are younger and without significant structural heart disease. Aetna CPB 0353 §VI.C explicitly states that 'documented contraindication or intolerance to all Class I and Class III antiarrhythmic agents, or first-line ablation per current ACC/AHA guidelines, satisfies the step-therapy requirement.' The 54-year-old male member has documented contraindications: (1) prior bronchospasm with flecainide trial 6 months ago requiring discontinuation, (2) QTc 478 ms at baseline precluding dofetilide and sotalol, and (3) hepatic steatosis with elevated transaminases contraindicating amiodarone. He is symptomatic with EHRA Class IIb palpitations and three documented ED visits for AF with RVR over the prior 6 months. Per CABANA trial subgroup analysis cited in the 2023 guideline §6.3.4, ablation as first-line therapy improves quality of life and reduces AF burden in this population. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within the 30-day deadline. Enclosed: the antiarrhythmic intolerance documentation, ECG showing prolonged QTc, hepatic panel, and EHRA symptom log. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.

Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Permanent Pacemaker (CPT 33207), Member with Complete AV Block (ICD-10 I44.2). We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0535 citing absent symptom or ECG documentation. The required documentation in fact exists in the inpatient telemetry record dated this admission and was omitted from the initial submission; we have enclosed the complete record. The 78-year-old female member experienced two witnessed syncopal episodes in the 48 hours prior to admission, documented by EMS run sheet and ED triage note. Inpatient continuous telemetry captured: (1) third-degree AV block with junctional escape rhythm at 32 bpm sustained over multiple hours, (2) a 4.8-second ventricular asystolic pause, and (3) symptomatic bradycardia with associated presyncope on standing. This satisfies Aetna CPB 0535 §V.A.1 Class I indications for permanent pacemaker, which list: symptomatic third-degree AV block, asystolic pauses ≥3 seconds while awake, and escape rhythm <40 bpm — all three of which are present here. The ACC/AHA/HRS 2018 Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay Guideline §3.2.1 assigns Class I, LOE B-NR recommendation for permanent pacing in acquired third-degree AV block with symptoms, and §3.2.3 (Class I, LOE C-LD) supports pacing for asystolic pauses ≥3 seconds in awake patients. There is no reversible cause: TSH 2.4, no AV-nodal-blocking agents on the medication list, no Lyme exposure, and no recent cardiac surgery. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within 30 days. Enclosed: telemetry strips, EMS sheet, medication reconciliation, and the cardiology consult note. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.

Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Transthoracic Echocardiogram (CPT 93303), Member with Congenital Mitral Stenosis (ICD-10 Q23.4). We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0119 citing prior TTE within 90 days. The repeat study is medically necessary under the documented-clinical-change exception in Aetna CPB 0119 §III.B.2. Since the prior TTE, the 28-year-old female member experienced two distinct clinical events: (1) new-onset paroxysmal atrial fibrillation captured on Holter monitor 18 days ago with rates to 156 bpm, and (2) new-onset hemoptysis (small volume, two episodes) with chest imaging negative for pulmonary embolism or pneumonia. Both events strongly suggest progression of mitral stenosis severity in this congenital substrate. Per ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §5.3.2 (Class I, LOE C-LD), repeat TTE is indicated when new symptoms develop in patients with known valvular disease, irrespective of routine surveillance intervals. The 2020 guideline §5.2 further notes that progression of mitral stenosis in young adults with congenital substrate can be more rapid than the rheumatic adult population, justifying early reassessment. Aetna CPB 0119 §III.B.2 specifically lists 'new arrhythmia in patient with known valvular disease' and 'hemoptysis suspicious for pulmonary venous hypertension' as covered indications for repeat TTE. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within 30 days. Enclosed: Holter report documenting AF, ED note describing hemoptysis, chest imaging, and prior TTE for comparison. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.
citations: ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §5.3.2 Class I

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement (CPT 33405), Member with Rheumatic Aortic Stenosis (ICD-10 I06.0). We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0286 citing mild stenosis (AVA 1.4 cm²) without symptoms. The submitted AVA measurement reflects an outdated study; current echocardiographic data establishes severe AS with clear symptoms. The most recent TTE dated 21 days prior to this submission documents: AVA 0.9 cm² (severe), mean transvalvular gradient 44 mmHg, peak velocity 4.4 m/s, dimensionless index 0.22, and LV ejection fraction 48% (down from 58% on prior study). These values satisfy CPB 0286 §III.B severity criteria (AVA <1.0 cm² OR mean gradient ≥40 mmHg OR peak velocity ≥4.0 m/s; the member meets all three). The patient is symptomatic: NYHA Class III dyspnea, two episodes of exertional presyncope, and recurrent angina on minimal exertion — satisfying CPB 0286 §III.A symptom criteria. The decline in LVEF from 58% to 48% additionally meets the §III.A.3 criterion of LV dysfunction in the setting of severe AS. ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §3.2.3 assigns Class I, LOE B-NR recommendation for SAVR in symptomatic severe AS, and the same guideline §3.2.4 supports SAVR in asymptomatic severe AS with declining LVEF <50% (Class I, LOE B-NR). The earlier 'mild stenosis' finding appears to have been based on a study performed 14 months ago and does not reflect current disease state. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within 30 days. Enclosed: the most recent TTE, symptom log, and ACC/AHA appropriateness criteria. We respectfully request the denial be overturned.
citations: ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §3.2.3 Class I
ACC/AHA 2020 Valvular Heart Disease Guideline §3.2.4 Class I

appeal_text: Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Diagnostic Cardiac Catheterization (CPT 93458), Member with Unspecified Angina (ICD-10 I20.9) — Timely-Filing Appeal. We request reconsideration of the denial issued on the basis of untimely claim submission for our 66-year-old male member. Aetna's notice asserts that the claim was received outside the 180-day initial filing window per the member's plan document. We respectfully submit that the claim was in fact timely under three independent grounds — statutory tolling, constructive receipt, and good cause — any one of which is sufficient to overturn the denial. First, statutory tolling: per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(f)(2)(iii)(A), a claim filing deadline is tolled when the plan fails to provide a determination within prescribed timeframes. The original prior-authorization request submitted on day 12 post-service did not receive a determination until day 47, well beyond the 30-day pre-service determination window under 29 CFR 2560.503-1(f)(2)(iii). This 35-day plan-side delay must be added back to the member's filing window. Second, constructive receipt: USPS Certified Mail tracking #7019-1640-0001-XXXX-XXXX confirms the claim was received at Aetna's claims-processing PO Box on day 168 post-service — clearly within the 180-day window. Aetna's internal scan timestamp of day 183 reflects mailroom processing delay on the plan's side, not member non-compliance, and under the mailbox rule and the constructive-receipt doctrine, the date of carrier delivery controls. Third, good cause: the member's provider submitted documentation of good cause under Aetna CPB §General Provisions. The member was hospitalized for an unrelated acute condition (urosepsis with septic shock requiring ICU admission, days 165–179) and was medically unable to follow up on claim status during the relevant window. Hospital discharge summary, ICU census reports, and bedside Glasgow Coma Scale scores ranging from 9 to 12 during the relevant period are enclosed. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(g)(1)(iii), the plan must apply reasonable claims procedures and may not enforce procedural deadlines in a manner that conflicts with the reasonable-claims-procedure standard; rigid application of a filing deadline against documented constructive receipt plus medically incapacitating illness is per se unreasonable and violates the regulation. The underlying cardiac catheterization remains medically necessary under Aetna CPB 0353 §IV.B (high pretest probability with typical angina), as the member presents with typical anginal symptoms, multiple cardiac risk factors, and a calculated PROMISE pretest probability of 62%. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file this appeal within the 30-day reconsideration window. Enclosed: USPS Certified Mail tracking record, hospital discharge summary documenting ICU stay, ICU GCS trend, the original prior-authorization timestamp documentation, and the underlying cath request with PROMISE score worksheet. We respectfully request the denial be overturned on the timely-filing ground and the underlying claim adjudicated on the merits.

Re: Adverse Benefit Determination — Stress Echocardiogram (CPT 93350), Member with Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure (ICD-10 I50.32) — Policy-Ambiguity Appeal. We request reconsideration of the denial under Aetna CPB 0119. The denial letter cites §III.A as the basis for non-coverage, asserting that stress echocardiography is 'not indicated in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction absent suspected coronary disease.' We respectfully submit that the policy language is materially ambiguous, and the ambiguity must be construed in favor of the member under the doctrine of contra proferentem as applied to ERISA plan documents (Glenn v. MetLife and progeny). Specifically, CPB 0119 §III.A states stress echo is covered for 'evaluation of suspected ischemic heart disease,' while §III.C states it is covered for 'assessment of diastolic function and exercise tolerance in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.' These two provisions overlap in our member's case — a 71-year-old woman with HFpEF (LVEF 58%), exertional dyspnea, and risk factors for CAD (diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia) — and §III.C plainly supports coverage. The denial letter's interpretation reads §III.A as an exclusion of §III.C, contradicting the plain text. ACC/AHA 2022 Heart Failure Guideline §7.3.2 (Class IIa, LOE B-NR) recommends stress echocardiography in HFpEF to assess for ischemic contribution and to characterize exercise-induced diastolic dysfunction. The 2022 Universal Definition of Heart Failure further establishes stress echo as the preferred modality when both questions (ischemia and diastolic reserve) are clinically relevant, as here. Where two reasonable readings of plan language exist, the reading favoring coverage controls. Per 29 CFR 2560.503-1(h) we file within 30 days. We respectfully request reconsideration of the denial under both the §III.C plain-text reading and, in the alternative, the contra proferentem doctrine.
citations: ACC/AHA 2022 Heart Failure Guideline §7.3.2 Class IIa
